scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE Civil Judge (Senior Division)
AT Bengaluru

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT WITH COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute this suit. The Defendant is a person sui juris and can be sued. The suit property is situated within the territorial limits of this Court, and the cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court. This suit relates to immovable property and is governed by Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. The suit is filed in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it, and the valuation is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court.

LIMITATION

2. The suit is within limitation under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

FACTS OF THE AGREEMENT

3. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff entered into an Agreement to Sell with the Defendant for the purchase of Residential Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft. The total consideration agreed upon was Rs. 85,00,000/-.
4. On the date of the agreement, 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant via RTGS (UTR: HDFC20240110987654). The balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was agreed to be paid on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution of the Sale Deed.
5. The Agreement to Sell is reduced in writing and signed by both parties, as marked as Annexure A. The proof of payment of the advance amount is supported by Annexure B.

PLAINTIFF'S READINESS AND WILLINGNESS

6. The Plaintiff has always been and continues to be ready and willing to perform her part of the contract. She has arranged the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- and keeps it available for payment against the execution of the Sale Deed.
7. All necessary steps have been taken to perform obligations, including keeping the funds ready and offering to complete the transaction within the stipulated timeline. No default has occurred on any term of the agreement and the Plaintiff remains capable of fulfilling the financial obligations required for the completion of the sale.

DEFENDANT'S REFUSAL TO PERFORM

8. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant sent a letter to the Plaintiff refusing to execute the Sale Deed, claiming he had received a higher offer for the suit property. This refusal is marked as Annexure C.
9. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the Sale Deed as per the agreement. The Defendant refused to comply with the notice. The legal notice and postal receipt are supported by Annexure D. The Defendant's failure to execute the Sale Deed constitutes a breach of the contract.

LEGAL BASIS

10. The Plaintiff is entitled to specific performance under Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, as the contract is in writing and signed. The Plaintiff has performed and continues to perform the conditions required under Section 16 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963.
11. The contract is valid and enforceable under Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. The Plaintiff seeks relief under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 for compensation if applicable.
12. The Plaintiff claims rights under Section 53A of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882 regarding part performance where applicable. The suit is maintainable under Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 as it relates to immovable property. The relief sought is not barred under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 as the contract is for transfer of immovable property.

CAUSE OF ACTION

13. The cause of action first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant refused to execute the Sale Deed despite the Plaintiff's readiness to perform.
14. The cause of action is continuing as the Defendant continues to refuse to perform his obligations. The suit is filed within the limitation period prescribed under Article 54 of the Limitation Act, 1963, being within three years from the date of refusal.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

15. The total suit valuation is Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty Five Lakhs Only) for the purpose of jurisdiction and court fee.
16. The court fee paid is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} computed under the {{COURT_FEE_ACT_NAME}}. The plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee.

PRAYER

17. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:
(a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024 directing the Defendant to execute the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff for the suit property;
(b) Direct the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff if not already in possession;
(c) In the alternative, award compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 in lieu of or in addition to specific performance, at such amount as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(d) Award pre-suit interest if contract, usage, or substantive law permits, and award pendente lite and future interest as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(e) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff;
(f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF SUIT PROPERTY

{{SCHEDULE_OF_SUIT_PROPERTY -- Address, Survey/Plot No., Extent/Area, Boundaries (East/West/North/South)}}

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt with UTR: HDFC20240110987654

Annexure C — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

Annexure D — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 17 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
                                    {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
                                    Mobile: {{ADVOCATE_PHONE}}
                                    Email: {{ADVOCATE_EMAIL}}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

C.S. No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A Company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A Limited Liability Partnership,
Having its office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT UNDER SECTION 73 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The suit is valued above the specified value as prescribed under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

2. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute this suit. The Defendant is a person sui juris and can be sued in its own name. The registered offices of both parties are located in Bengaluru, and the contract was executed and was to be performed within this city.

3. This Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to try this suit under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, as the cause of action arose within the territorial limits of this Court. The suit is filed in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

4. The subject matter is a commercial dispute of specified value. The Plaintiff has complied with the requirements of Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 regarding pre-institution mediation prior to filing this suit.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

5. The Plaintiff states that pre-institution mediation was conducted as required under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The mediation was conducted through the designated mediation center and the outcome certificate is attached as Annexure F.

LIMITATION

6. The suit is within limitation under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

FACTS OF THE CASE

7. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a written Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system. The total contract value was agreed at Rs. 45,00,000/-. The Plaintiff is a company registered under the Companies Act, represented by its Director Priya Menon, and the Defendant is a limited liability partnership, represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh. Both parties are competent to contract under law.

8. Pursuant to the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid the first milestone payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant on 01.02.2024 via NEFT bearing UTR No. ICIC20240201456789. The Defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system by 01.05.2024. The Plaintiff has fully performed its payment obligations under the contract.

9. The terms of the Agreement, proof of payment, and subsequent communications are documented in Annexure A (Agreement), Annexure B (NEFT receipt), and Annexure C (Email correspondence). The Plaintiff remains ready and willing to perform its remaining obligations subject to the Defendant's performance.

BREACH OF CONTRACT AND PARTICULARS

10. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module of the ERP system by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024. Despite the lapse of the stipulated time, the Defendant did not complete the development work nor communicate any valid reason for the delay.

11. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant abandoned the project without delivering any module and without returning the advance payment received from the Plaintiff. This constitutes a material breach of the essential terms of the Agreement.

12. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 calling upon the Defendant to remedy the breach and refund the advance. The Defendant has failed to comply with the notice or respond substantively, as evidenced by Annexure E (Legal Notice).

LOSS AND DAMAGE

13. The Plaintiff has suffered actual loss quantified at Rs. 20,00,000/-, being the advance payment made to the Defendant which remains unrefunded despite the abandonment of the project. This amount is directly attributable to the Defendant's failure to perform.

14. The Plaintiff further suffered consequential damages amounting to Rs. 12,00,000/- due to the delay in operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system. This loss is assessed in the auditor's report attached as Annexure D.

15. The Plaintiff took reasonable steps to mitigate the loss by issuing the legal notice dated 01.07.2024 and engaging an auditor to assess the damages. The total claim for damages is Rs. 32,00,000/-, excluding interest.

LEGAL BASIS

16. The Plaintiff is entitled to relief under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides compensation for loss or damage caused by the breach of contract. The Defendant's failure to deliver the module by 01.05.2024 invokes Section 55 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 regarding failure to perform at the agreed time.

17. The parties are bound to perform their respective promises under Section 37 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. The Plaintiff pleads that no subsequent agreement has replaced or discharged the original contract, precluding any defence of novation or accord and satisfaction.

18. The Plaintiff has taken specific steps to minimise loss after the breach, satisfying the duty to mitigate. The Defendant is liable to compensate the Plaintiff for the natural and direct consequences of the breach as per the doctrine of damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

CAUSE OF ACTION

19. The cause of action first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and failed to deliver the module or refund the advance. The right to sue accrued upon the Defendant's express failure to perform its contractual obligations.

20. The suit is filed within the limitation period prescribed by Article 55 of the Limitation Act, 1963, which allows three years from the date of breach. The breach is a single event of abandonment and non-performance, not a continuing breach.

21. The Plaintiff asserts that the suit is timely and maintainable within the statutory limitation period.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

22. For the purpose of jurisdiction and court fee, the suit is valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only). This valuation represents the total claim for refund and consequential damages.

23. The requisite court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on the plaint under the {{COURT_FEE_ACT_NAME}}. The plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee.

24. The valuation is stated to determine the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Commercial Court and the applicable court fee liability.

PRAYER

25. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:
(a) Award damages in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) towards refund of advance and consequential loss;
(b) Award interest at 18% per annum on the claimed amount from the date of breach till realisation;
(c) Award future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit from the date of decree till realisation under Section 34 CPC;
(d) Award costs of the suit including advocate's fees to the Plaintiff;
(e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF DAMAGES / PARTICULARS OF DAMAGES

The Plaintiff claims the following heads of damages with computation methodology:

(i) Actua
